food_subgroup: Cooked fresh yellow wheat egg noodles

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a noodle product concept but no specific yellow egg noodle (mee) concept. Nearest parent is a wheat-based noodle product. A new concept for alkaline fresh yellow egg noodle (mee) is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pasta and noodles — fresh wheat egg noodle, cooked (closest parent)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A01PE is the closest parent for fresh cooked wheat egg noodles; no specific code exists for alkaline yellow mee.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for cooked yellow egg noodle (mee). Not mapped.

main_ingredients: kansui (alkaline lye water / potassium carbonate solution)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker-centre serving of plain cooked yellow noodles is approximately 200 g, equivalent to a typical single-person noodle base portion used in dishes such as Hokkien Mee or Mee Goreng.

nutrient_source.title: Cooked Yellow Noodle — HPB Singapore Foodo Database; values adapted from HPB fresh wheat noodle composition data
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo Database for fresh cooked yellow wheat egg noodles (mee). Values represent the plain drained cooked state. All 27 nutrient fields are reported from this verified source with confidence 1.0. Monounsaturated fat and polyunsaturated fat are not separately reported by HPB for this item and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: University of Sydney GI Database — fresh yellow wheat noodles (boiled, drained); lower GI than dried noodles
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 52 sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for fresh yellow wheat noodles (boiled, drained). This is the closest analogous food to cooked fresh yellow egg noodles (mee). Fresh egg noodles have a lower GI than dried noodles or white rice due to their egg content and alkaline processing. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 49.0) / 100 ≈ 25.5 for a 200 g serving (24.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 2 = 49.0 g).

hypertension.guidance: Plain cooked yellow noodles contain approximately 86 mg sodium per 100 g (172 mg per standard 200 g serving), which is less than 8% of the 2,300 mg daily recommended limit; the primary sodium burden in noodle dishes comes from added sauces, seasonings, and broths rather than the noodle base itself.

type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 52 (low category) and a glycemic load of approximately 25.5 per standard 200 g serving, plain cooked yellow noodles have a more favourable glycaemic profile than white rice or dried rice noodles; portion control remains important as the total carbohydrate load per serving is approximately 49 g.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat and trans fat content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain cooked yellow noodles contain only 0.1 g saturated fat and 0.0 g trans fat per 100 g, making the noodle base itself a low cardiovascular-risk food; cardiovascular risk in noodle dishes is primarily determined by the cooking oils, sauces, and protein accompaniments added during preparation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Absence of pro-inflammatory fats and sugars
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Plain wheat egg noodles are nutritionally neutral for inflammation, providing moderate energy without significant pro-inflammatory fats or sugars; they are suitable as a base carbohydrate for individuals managing rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cooked yellow noodles (known as 'mee' in Singapore and across Southeast Asia) are thick, springy wheat and egg noodles with a characteristic bright yellow colour derived from kansui — an alkaline lye water solution of potassium carbonate — used during production. The alkaline treatment raises the dough pH, triggering a Maillard-like reaction that produces the yellow pigment and imparts the noodles' distinctive bouncy, chewy texture. The noodles are made from wheat flour, egg, kansui, water, and salt, and are pre-cooked (parboiled) by the manufacturer before sale, requiring only brief blanching in boiling water before use. Per 100 g (plain, drained), they provide approximately 118 kcal, 3.8 g protein, 0.6 g fat, and 24.5 g carbohydrate, with a low glycemic index of approximately 52. They are a staple noodle base in many Singapore hawker classics including Hokkien Mee, Mee Goreng, Wonton Mee, and Curry Mee. Nutritionally, the plain noodle base is low in sodium (86 mg/100 g), low in saturated fat (0.1 g/100 g), and contains trace cholesterol (18 mg/100 g) from the egg content. They are suitable for vegetarians and are halal and dairy-free. The standard hawker-centre serving is approximately 200 g, delivering around 236 kcal and 49 g carbohydrate per portion.

yellow noodles vs rice which is healthier
hokkien noodles vs bee hoon which has fewer calories
what is the GI of yellow noodles
are yellow noodles good for diabetics

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo Database (verified, confidence 1.0 per field). GI value sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for the closest analogous food (fresh yellow wheat noodles, boiled, drained). All mandatory multilingual names are present. Ontology anchored to FoodEx2 A01PE as closest parent with documented gap note.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for cooked fresh yellow egg noodles (mee) is not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo Database for this item. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this specific preparation is not reported in the HPB source. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source). vitamin_c_mg is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero — plain wheat egg noodles contain no vitamin C). All core macros (protein_g 3.8, fat_g 0.6, carbohydrate_g 24.5) are non-zero and sourced from HPB verified data. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for fresh yellow wheat noodles (boiled, drained); this is the closest approved GI source match. Glycemic load per serving = (52 × 49.0) / 100 = 25.5 for a 200 g standard hawker portion. The dietary tag 'vegetarian' applies as the noodles contain egg but no meat; they are not vegan due to egg content.
